problem_summary: 2008 MCM Problem B (discrete): design an algorithm that constructs UNIQUE-solution Sudoku puzzles at four selectable difficulty levels (easy/medium/hard/evil) while minimizing algorithmic complexity, AND develop a metric to objectively measure puzzle difficulty. The team's solution couples (1) a custom, fast difficulty metric — the Weighted Normalized Ease Function (WNEF) — empirically calibrated against published puzzles, with (2) a two-stage generator (backtracking solution build + "hole-punching" mask removal) that uses the WNEF as the objective function and simulated annealing to reach hard targets.
models_used: Weighted Normalized Ease Function (WNEF) — a custom difficulty metric: bin every empty cell by its number of candidates |X?| into a 9-bin choice histogram, convolve with a weight function, then normalize by w(1)*|E(P)| so that removing givens can't trivially inflate the score (eqs. 1-4)
Choice histogram / candidate-counting via constraint elimination (count candidates per empty cell from its row, column, and 3x3 block) as the raw feature feeding the metric

Three competing weight functions compared empirically: exponential w_exp(n)=2^(9-n), squared w_sq(n)=(10-n)^2, linear w_lin(n)=(10-n); all gave strong negative correlation, so exponential was chosen arbitrarily
Backtracking search to generate a completed valid board, recast as a 1D list of rows (fill row-by-row, backtrack a row at a time) maintaining per-column and per-cluster value sets

Mask removal ('punching holes') in two variants: Random Masking (remove cells in random order, keep if still uniquely solvable -> minimal puzzle) and Tuned Masking (strategy-driven removal aimed at a target WNEF)

Simulated annealing as the optimization layer: when masking hits a WNEF floor, add some givens back and re-optimize, with state-saving to revert to the lowest-WNEF board found, to push toward harder (lower-WNEF) targets

Uniqueness testing as a hybrid solver: a 'fast' logical solver using only Naked Single + Hidden Single, falling back to a 'slow' recursive brute-force (try each candidate, count solutions) bounded by a max number of allowed guesses
Human solving-technique catalog formalized with set notation (Naked Pair/Triplet, Hidden Pair/Triplet, Multi-Line) with correctness proofs by contradiction — used to motivate the difficulty model

Gaussian likelihood classifier: each difficulty level modeled as a Normal(mu_d, sigma_d^2) over WNEF (calibrated means/variances in Table 1), with a pdf() used to classify/interpolate a puzzle's difficulty
Welch-style two-sample Student's t-test (unequal-variance t-statistic) used twice: to prove the four control-difficulty WNEF means are distinct, and to prove annealing significantly lowers WNEF
Empirical complexity profiling: a PHP RAII-destructor Profiler timing functions, reporting normalized time t-hat = t/t0 against the random-fill baseline
key_techniques: Inventing a bespoke, cheap-to-compute metric (WNEF) instead of reusing slow rule-battery difficulty scores — the whole metric is O(1): bounded by 1,944 cell lookups (24 neighbors x 81 cells), enabling thousands of evaluations during tuning

Smart normalization to defeat a degenerate failure mode: raw weighted-ease strictly increases as you remove givens (more empty cells), so they divide by w(1)*|E(P)| (the all-determined ceiling) to make the metric meaningful — a self-aware fix of their own model

Empirical calibration against a large labeled control corpus: 4 sources, ~4,400+ puzzles (WebSudoku, Games World of Sudoku, 2000 GNOME 'hard', 2365 'top2365' evil) spanning 4 author-labeled difficulties — turning a subjective notion into a measurable, validated scale

Using the metric AS the optimization objective for generation (metric-driven generation): the generator removes cells specifically to drive WNEF to a target band, closing the loop between measurement and construction

Hybrid fast/slow uniqueness test with a guess budget ('reject if we'd have to guess more than twice') — trades a few false negatives for large speedups, exactly the right engineering call for a generate-and-test inner loop
Reframing the 2D board as a 1D sequence of rows to shrink the backtracking state and constraint bookkeeping (only track columns + the 3 intersecting clusters)
Statistical hypothesis testing to substantiate every key claim quantitatively (levels are distinct at 99.5%; annealing helps at 99.95%) rather than asserting them
Explicit scope-fencing: a 'we do NOT aim to...' list (no forced solving method, no symmetry, not the world's hardest generator) that bounds the project and pre-empts judge criticism

Honest weakness analysis tied to the model's core assumption — they identify that Swordfish/XY-Wing puzzles break the 'all choices equally hard' assumption and even explain the WNEF floor that forced them to make 'Evil' easier than published evil puzzles
why_it_won: "From a judge's view this paper wins on three axes. First, it nails BOTH deliverables the problem demanded — a validated difficulty METRIC and a working tunable GENERATOR — and it ties them together: the metric is the generator's objective function, which reads as a single coherent idea rather than two bolted-on parts. Second, it backs claims with evidence: a large calibrated control set, correlation plots, a Gaussian per-difficulty model, and TWO Student's t-tests that quantitatively prove (a) the four difficulty levels are statistically distinct and (b) annealing measurably helps — judges reward verified, quantitative claims over assertions. Third, it shows genuine modeling maturity: it recognizes and FIXES a degenerate behavior in its own metric (normalization), reasons explicitly about complexity (proving the metric is O(1) and the generator runs at worst ~20x random), and writes a candid weaknesses section that correctly names the assumption it violates (Swordfish/XY-Wing puzzles) and even explains its own 'Evil' floor. The combination of NP-completeness framing, rigorous notation, statistical validation, runnable Java/PHP code, and disciplined scope control signals a team that did real, self-critical science — which is exactly the Outstanding profile."
section_highlights: "Strong, consistent mathematical scaffolding up front: a full Terminology/Notation section, a formal indexing system with closed-form index maps c(i,j), i(c,k), j(c,k), and a one-line formal statement of the Sudoku constraint in quantifier notation — this lets later claims and the solving-technique proofs (Naked/Hidden Pair/Triplet, Multi-Line, each proved by contradiction) be stated rigorously. The Metric Design -> Calibration -> Generator flow is logically staged: define WNEF mathematically (eqs 1-4), calibrate it on real puzzles with histograms and correlation plots (Figs 10-12, Table 1), THEN use it to drive the generator. Detailed step-numbered pseudocode is given for all four core routines (board generation, random masking, tuned masking, uniqueness testing), and complexity is analyzed per-component with concrete worst/average/best cases (e.g. 9^27 worst vs ~6561 average for board gen; 81^3 for random fill). Results are quantified with tables (Table 1 control means/variances, Table 2 pre/post-annealing) and figures (running time vs WNEF on log2 scale, WNEF vs allowed failures). A clean Strengths/Weaknesses section and a Conclusions section that restates the confidence levels (99.5%, 99.95%) close it out. Full Java + PHP source is appended, making every claim reproducible."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Core modeling assumption is fragile: WNEF assumes 'difficulty is roughly proportional to the number of candidate choices' and that all choices are equally hard. This is provably false for puzzles solvable only via advanced techniques (Swordfish, XY-Wing), which can be hard yet have a high (easy-looking) WNEF — the authors admit this caps the metric's valid regime
WNEF measures only the INITIAL candidate distribution; it ignores solving-path depth/length, so two puzzles with identical starting histograms but very different logical chains score the same

The generator has a WNEF 'floor' — its random/probabilistic core rarely produces very-low-WNEF (truly evil) boards, forcing the team to define 'Evil' EASIER than published evil puzzles. This is a real failure to fully meet the implicit difficulty ceiling
Tuned masking suffers 'bleeding': difficulty distributions of adjacent tunings overlap on their tails, so a 'hard' request can leak into 'evil', requiring regeneration with a bad worst-case time
The exponential weight function was chosen 'arbitrarily' among three — defensible (all correlate) but means the specific weights {256..1} aren't optimized or independently justified

Statistical i.i.d. assumption across heterogeneous sources (WebSudoku vs GNOME vs top2365) is asserted, not tested; pooling author-labeled difficulties across vendors risks label-noise that the t-tests don't account for

The transcribed paper has notable typos/garbles (e.g., the pdf normalization comment writes 1/(2*pi*sigma^2) and an exp argument missing a square; 'Section 19' cross-reference; 'WebSudoku Easy' figure cross-refs are inconsistent) — sloppy proofreading that, while not fatal, is the kind of polish gap judges notice
reusable_patterns: Invent a cheap surrogate metric and make it the optimizer's objective: when the 'true' quality measure (here, real solving difficulty) is expensive, design a fast proxy (O(1) WNEF), validate it correlates with the truth on labeled data, then optimize against the proxy. Generalizes to any 'measure-then-construct' MCM problem

Calibrate a model against a labeled control set and PROVE separation with a two-sample t-test: collect real-world examples binned by the target label, compute your metric, and run pairwise Welch t-tests to show the metric statistically distinguishes the classes. Reusable for any classification/ranking deliverable

Normalize away a degenerate monotonicity: if a raw score trivially grows/shrinks with problem size, divide by its theoretical extreme to recover a meaningful 0-1 quantity — and explicitly call out why (judges love seeing you catch your own metric's flaw)

Generate-and-test with a budgeted hybrid verifier: pair a fast incomplete check (logic-only solver) with a bounded exact fallback (brute force capped at k guesses) to get near-exact correctness at a fraction of the cost in a tight inner loop

Two-stage 'build-then-degrade' construction: build a complete valid object, then greedily remove elements while a feasibility invariant (unique solution) holds, optionally with annealing to escape local optima — a template for many discrete construction/optimization problems

Use simulated annealing specifically to break through an objective 'floor' that greedy removal can't, with state-saving to keep the best solution found — and then t-test that the annealing actually helped
Model each output class as a Gaussian over your metric (mu, sigma per class) and classify via likelihood/pdf — a lightweight, defensible classifier when you already have per-class metric statistics

Pre-empt criticism with an explicit 'non-goals' list and a candid weaknesses section that names the exact assumption your model violates — disciplined scope control plus intellectual honesty reads as Outstanding-tier maturity
Profile empirically and report HARDWARE-NORMALIZED time (t/t0 vs a baseline algorithm) so complexity claims are reproducible and machine-independent
Front-load rigorous notation/indexing so that later proofs, pseudocode, and complexity counts can be stated precisely and unambiguously
